title: Emergency Guide: My Dog Ate Fresh Grapes
meta_desc: Grapes are highly toxic to dogs and can cause kidney failure. This is a high-priority emergency. Contact a veterinarian or poison control immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Grape and raisin ingestion is a high-priority medical emergency for dogs that can cause sudden, acute kidney failure. Seek immediate veterinary care if your dog consumes any amount of grapes, as even a small quantity can be fatal.

What is grape toxicity in dogs and why is it dangerous?

Grape and raisin toxicity is a serious condition in dogs that can lead to sudden, acute kidney failure. While the exact substance causing the reaction is not fully understood, even a small amount can be fatal to some dogs. Unlike other toxins where the effect is dose-dependent, some dogs show extreme sensitivity to just one or two grapes.

Is grape ingestion in dogs a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level is High; if your dog has ingested fresh grapes or raisins, treat it as a medical emergency.
  • Do not wait for symptoms like vomiting, lethargy, or changes in urination to appear, as these indicate kidney damage has already begun.
  • Immediate veterinary intervention is the best way to prevent permanent organ damage.

How can a photo help my veterinarian triage grape toxicity?

  • Provide a photo of the grapes or the original packaging to help the professional confirm the fruit type and estimate the volume consumed.
  • If your dog has already vomited, take a photo of the contents to help the vet determine if any grape skins or fruit matter were successfully expelled.
  • Visual information allows a triage nurse or remote veterinarian to assess the severity of the situation more accurately.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ingestion of grapes can result in acute renal failure in some dogs, with vomiting and/or diarrhea often developing within 6-12 hours. Other clinical signs include lethargy, anorexia, abdominal pain, weakness, dehydration, polydipsia, and tremors. Serum creatinine levels tend to rise early and disproportionately compared with serum urea nitrogen levels. Oliguric or anuric renal failure can develop within 24-72 hours of exposure. Prompt decontamination is recommended for significant ingestions; emesis may be induced with 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg, not exceeding 45 mL), followed by activated charcoal. For large ingestions, or if vomiting/diarrhea has spontaneously developed within 12 hours, aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended, with monitoring of renal function and fluid balance. The exact mechanism of toxicity is unknown, and a clear dose-response relationship has not been determined.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How many grapes are toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: There is no known safe amount. Because some dogs are hyper-sensitive, a single grape can cause kidney failure in a large dog, while others might eat several without immediate issues. All ingestions should be treated as emergencies.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of grape poisoning?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include vomiting, diarrhea, loss of appetite, lethargy, abdominal pain, and decreased urination. However, these signs may not appear for several hours, at which point internal damage may already be severe.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: You should never induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian or a pet poison control expert, as there are risks of aspiration if not performed correctly.
